content: On Bhadra 23, I was present at the ministry and had coordinated with the heads of security agencies in meetings. Information received through the Chief District Officer was shared with the Secretary and the Minister. On that same day, after receiving information through the Chief District Officer of Kathmandu that curfew had been imposed and that arrangements needed to be made to dispatch the Nepal Army quickly, I immediately relayed that information to the Minister and the Secretary.

On Bhadra 24, I went to Baluwatar and coordinated with the Chief Secretary and heads of security agencies to prepare and publish a press release. On Bhadra 24, although demonstrators entered Singha Durbar and set fire to the Ministry of Home Affairs, most documents related to my division remained safe.

The responsibility for the human casualties caused when dozens of people were randomly shot at almost simultaneously on Bhadra 23 lies with responsible individuals of the then government, from commanders deployed in the field to police officers at various command levels  and when deciding to take immediate action against them, it is also necessary to pay attention to the potential impact this may have on security in the coming days.

My assessment is that there was a lack of adequate information and analysis regarding the security weaknesses related to the incident. Regarding the matter of attempts to release Rabi Lamichhane from Nakkhu Prison, I received a phone call from the jailer and I had informed the Secretary of that matter.

I have reached the conclusion that the government must invest in information collection, analysis, and resource management for administrative reform. My view is that institutional reform is necessary to improve relations with the younger generation  providing regular dialogue, technology-friendly services, and predictable services.

Finally, I have pointed out the need for the Ministry of Home Affairs to reduce the tendency to put unnecessary pressure on security agencies in the name of Civil Supremacy, to work with the country and people at the center, and to take necessary steps to prevent such movements from recurring in the coming days.
18. Statement of Then Chief District Officer of Kathmandu District, Joint Secretary Chhabilal Rijal:

I have been serving as Chief District Officer of Kathmandu District with thirty-six years of experience in the civil service. I have previously successfully served as Chief District Officer at District Administration Offices in Surkhet, Jhapa, and Tanahun.

On 2082/5/23, three joint applications were received at the office  one in the name of Sudan Gurung of an organization called 'Hami Nepal', one in the names of Ankit Malla and Khemraj Saud, and one in the names of Sabal Gautam and Purushottam Yadav  requesting permission to hold a peaceful demonstration in the Maitighar Mandala area. During discussions with those who came to submit the applications, oral agreement was reached that demonstrations would be held only in areas other than prohibited zones and that the organizers would conduct the program peacefully.

After the Gen Z movement was announced, through a District Security Committee (DSC) meeting, I directed security arrangements to be further strengthened with the objective of preventing a repeat of the security lapse that had occurred at Tinkune on the previous Chaitra 15, and that relevant agencies coordinate with their line agencies in a timely manner for additional troop deployment if necessary.

Based on information received from the police administration, intelligence apparatus, and district security agencies, the movement was understood as a program to express dissatisfaction regarding social media-related matters, and estimating that it would be peaceful in its initial phase, possible routes, timing, estimated number of participants, and sensitive areas were identified, the 2082 Integrated Special Security Plan was approved, and security personnel were deployed accordingly.

Due to the assumption that the capital would remain peaceful on Bhadra 23, a National Assembly session was scheduled to be held in the Federal Parliament building on that same day, and the Honorable President's attendance at the National Assembly Hall program had also been confirmed.

On 2082/5/22, I and the District Police Chief attended the Central Security Committee meeting held at the Ministry of Home Affairs at around 6:00 PM in the evening and gave a briefing on the demonstration. A suggestion was given to rescind the decision to close social media platforms not registered in Nepal. When the Inspector General of Nepal Police asked for a directive on the line of action  what to do if it becomes necessary to use force in tomorrow's demonstration  the directive received was: to use minimum force sequentially in accordance with the principle of force escalation to bring the situation under control, but if even that fails to bring the situation under control and there is unruly activity such as violation of prohibited zones, rioting, vandalism, and arson, then to carefully use necessary force to bring the situation under control.

From 7:00 AM on Bhadra 23, I had set up a District Security Committee command center in my own office chamber and was sitting together with officials monitoring the situation. The situation was normal until approximately 10:15 AM. After the peaceful demonstration that had started from Maitighar broke through the police barricade in front of Everest Hotel via Bijulibazar Bridge and moved forward, seeing that the situation could go out of control, I called the Home Secretary to inform him and received a directive from him to 'use minimum force sequentially to control the situation.'

After the demonstrators advanced toward the Federal Parliament building and it appeared that peace would be disrupted, I called the Honorable Home Minister, the Chief Secretary, and the Home Secretary to inform them that curfew needed to be imposed. After receiving directives from all of them to bring the situation under control even if curfew needed to be imposed, on the basis of the District Security Committee's recommendation, a curfew order was issued to come into effect from 12:30 PM.

After learning that tension was escalating in the New Baneswhar area, at around 12:15 PM I also sent Armed Police Superintendent Jeevan K.C. there with the objective of maintaining peace. At around 12:00 noon, I received information that demonstrators from all four directions around the Parliament building were committing vandalism and arson and entering through the southeast, east, and southwest-facing gates.

As violence spread and serious risk to citizens' lives, property, and public and private assets became apparent, it was necessary to issue a curfew order as a last resort. After discussion with the District Security Committee, police, armed police, and Nepal Army, and after a serious review of the situation, a curfew order was issued in phases with limited time and area in accordance with Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 2028.

Even after the curfew was issued, I continuously directed security personnel not to lose patience, not to be discouraged by saying that additional security personnel including the army are coming, to use minimum force in dispersing crowds, to be mindful of children's rights and human rights, and not to lose composure.

Although I gave a verbal order to fire rubber bullets targeting below the knees, I had not given an order for the use of lethal force  i.e., to open direct fire. However, in that very tense situation, I heard through the police radio set  first the Inspector General of Police and then the Acting Chief of the Kathmandu Valley Police Office  give an order to 'open fire to bring the situation under control as necessary.'

Due to the extremely tense situation in the field, exact details of how many demonstrators were around the Parliament building, how much force was used, and how many were injured or killed could not be obtained in a timely manner. Reports were received that attackers armed with helmets and carrying stones, bricks, hammers, mallets, rods, chisels, and slingshots were advancing on security personnel.

When demonstrators tried to break through or climb over the walls to enter the Parliament building, I had received information from the District Police Chief and heads of other security agencies that gunfire occurred only inside the Parliament building premises  for the protection of parliamentarians, staff, and structures present there  and that no gunfire occurred outside elsewhere.

The Nepal Army's assistance was requested for effective implementation of the curfew, but due to obstruction by the demonstrators, the army could not reach the Parliament building in time. At around 2:15 PM, I received information that one demonstrator had died in front of the Parliament building.

According to security personnel, the demonstrators became aggressive and engaged in arson and vandalism from all four directions and attacked police. After the situation could not be brought under control even after using the principles of force  baton charge, tear gas, aerial fire, and rubber bullets  security personnel deployed according to the Special Security Force and Integrated Special Security Plan, with attention to the security of the Parliament building, parliamentarians and staff present there, Nepal Bank Limited, Nepal Law Commission, the Investment Board office, and a diesel reserve of approximately forty thousand liters, appear to have used force to immediately face the situation.

I directed that minimum force be used with composure in accordance with the Dual Objective Doctrine of protecting the Parliament building and also protecting the lives of the youth who had come out to demonstrate, and that only as a last resort were rubber bullets to be fired targeting below the knees.

After the events of Bhadra 23, 2082, a District Security Committee meeting was held at 10:30 PM that night, and with the objective of preventing the situation from deteriorating on the 24th, a decision was made to impose curfew from midnight 12:00 AM to 5:00 AM that morning, and security agencies were directed to exercise high vigilance to prevent looting, vandalism, and arson of public and private property.

On the morning of Bhadra 24, a meeting was again held at 7:30 AM and curfew was declared from 8:30 AM until further notice. On that same day, anger was directed at me on social media  rumors were spread that I had died, and threatening phone calls came saying my life would be taken  but I remained focused on work, thinking at least I might be able to protect the Vital Installations.

During discussions at the Ministry of Home Affairs, after receiving information that even Singha Durbar was not safe, at around 1:45 PM I went and stayed at the Nepal Army's Narasinghdal Gana. After the Prime Minister's resignation, the vacuum visible in the executive leadership of the state created further confusion and anger in the public, and despite the curfew, arson, looting, and attacks on government offices and private businesses occurred in various places.

Since the crowd was extremely large and the multi-centered attacks occurred almost simultaneously, it was not possible to provide effective security at all locations at once. The fatigue of security personnel, shortage of equipment, prolonged continuous duty, and the effect on morale also added to the challenges.

On Bhadra 24, since the Nepal Army did not timely take over prison duty, Nepal Police alone could not provide security, leading to the situation where 20 prisoners escaped from Dillibazar Prison and more than 3,500 prisoners escaped from the Central Prison in Sundhara.

Since the federal capital Kathmandu has constitutional bodies, offices and residences of very senior officials, an international airport, ministries, important structures, and even the headquarters of security agencies, in certain situations senior security officers and high-ranking officials' directives can be received directly by security personnel  which shows the reality that the Chief District Officer's role alone is not decisive in maintaining peace and security in the district.

Even while discharging all responsibilities of my official capacity with good intent and competence, I am deeply grieved by the news that circumstances beyond what the state apparatus had estimated arose, leading to the deaths of youth who participated in the demonstrations and dozens being injured. I did fulfill my role as a Chief District Officer, but it would not be just to hold me solely responsible on the grounds that circumstances arose beyond what the state apparatus had estimated  circumstances that required the head of government to resign and necessitated helicopter rescue to protect the lives of the Prime Minister and ministers.

Without deep study of who caused the circumstances in which citizens had no choice but to rebel, one cannot say that such a major event occurred only due to the weakness of one or two government officials  this is the result of the distortions, anomalies, injustice, and inequality that have existed in this country for a long time. I have also heard complaints from Nepal Police officers that they did not openly receive the expected cooperation from the Armed Police Force deployed in the field and other security personnel.

Finally, the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 must be taken as an abnormal, unexpected, and serious test for the state apparatus. Based on the information I received, legal authority, and assessment of the immediate circumstances, I made decisions prioritizing public interest, peace and security, and protection of citizens' lives and property. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, I recommend strengthening the early warning system, increasing crowd management training and equipment, strengthening inter-agency coordination, and maintaining political-administrative clarity.
19. Statement of Joint Secretary Lila Prasad Sharma, Director General of the Department of Prison Management:

Until the evening of Bhadra 23, according to regular reporting received from all prisons across the country, the situation was normal. However, on the afternoon of the 24th, after receiving information from Nakkhu Prison that the crowd around the prison was growing, that agitators were approaching, and that there were signs the prison could be targeted, I became alert. I had immediately coordinated with the Ministry of Home Affairs and the Chief District Officer of Lalitpur and given directives to strengthen security. However, amidst the widespread disorganized movement across the country, the excitement spreading through social media, and the limitations of the local security mechanism, a large crowd managed to enter Nakkhu Prison within just a few hours, rendering the department's security plans ineffective and pushing the situation out of control.

The attack, vandalism, and arson that began at Nakkhu Prison spread across the country within a few hours. The situation went out of control due to both the aggressive entry of outside groups and the spontaneous impulse of prisoners inside. Although the department was maintaining continuous contact with Chief District Officers, police, armed police, and the ministry and giving priority-based alertness directives, it appears that the nature and intensity of the movement put excessive pressure on local security personnel. Meanwhile, prisoners in various prisons began vandalism and destruction of records themselves, making the situation even more complex.

Within three days of the incident, similar nature events occurred in 34 prisons across the country. From 28 prisons, 14,554 prisoners and detainees escaped  of whom 13,585 were adults and 969 were juvenile offenders. In Nauwasta, Dhading, and Ramechhap, 10 prisoners died during security operations. In prisons including Nakkhu, Dillibazar, Makwanpur, Tanahun, and Kaski, arson occurred, vehicles were destroyed, records were burned, CCTV was completely demolished, office buildings were damaged, and prisoner housing blocks were also burned. Due to the burning of prison records, office machinery, and assets, the prison system across the country came to an immediate standstill.

Regarding the Rabi Lamichhane matter, which became a subject of public concern, I wish to clarify that the information received from Nakkhu Prison on the morning of the 24th about the risk from agitators was only for coordination between the department and the ministry. No order or signal to release him from prison was given at any stage by the department or ministry. All department activities were focused entirely on security strengthening. I only learned later that he had been taken out of the prison by the crowd when the crowd demolished the prison. Regarding the release letter that became public after the movement, the department only received information that the jailer had signed it under duress  that letter was not issued in any procedural, legal, or departmental capacity whatsoever.

Regarding questions raised about my role, I wish to clearly state that the department was continuously undertaking risk mitigation, security coordination, information flow, and directive work within the limits of available resources. I do not consider myself directly responsible for the damage; however, the structural weaknesses of the prison system and disorganized security management have been longstanding problems.

After the incident, the department immediately commenced rehabilitation work. Priority has been given to issuing public notices for escaped prisoners to return, monitoring through immigration, updating CRS records, preparing damage assessment and budget estimates, conducting a structural review of the security force, and forming search task forces in each district. Many prisoners have returned to prison, some have been arrested, but 4,841 prisoners and detainees are still at large. Since record management was weak, a decision has been made to make mandatory entry of three-generation details of all prisoners and to expand the information system.

Regarding the juvenile reform home, 5 deaths occurred at Nauwasta Reform Center. Since housing juvenile offenders above 18 years of age in the same location has been the primary challenge, a transfer program has been initiated in coordination between ministries. My conclusion is that commitments to prison reform are not possible without structural transformation. New laws and regulations, a modern record system, strong infrastructure, an emergency management plan, and an integrated command system in the security mechanism are indispensable. Seeking alternative measures without reforming the overall structure is not practical.

Finally, even as the events of Bhadra 24 expanded to a state of national anarchy beyond control, the department was continuously making coordination, directive, and rehabilitation efforts. I express my expectation that the Commission will identify the actual facts and provide guidance toward the necessary policy and structural reforms to make prison administration credible and capable in the future.
20. Statement of Saroj Kumar Sharma, Member Secretary of the Social Welfare Council:

Member Secretary of the Social Welfare Council, Saroj Kumar Sharma, in his statement presented before the Inquiry Commission regarding events related to the demonstrations called by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, has detailed the institutional situation of the council, the existing structure of NGO/INGO regulation, its weaknesses, practices, and policy challenges. He noted that he has been working as Member Secretary of the Social Welfare Council since Kartik 29, 2082, and provided information that currently more than approximately 58,000 national NGOs and 134 international NGOs are registered and affiliated with the council.

In his statement, he acknowledged that the council does not yet have a fully integrated national digital database of active NGOs/INGOs. He openly acknowledged the situation that despite clear provisions under Article 51 of the Constitution requiring adoption of a single-window policy, and legal provisions under various sections of the Social Welfare Act, 2049, preventing direct entry through ministries, those provisions have not been fully implemented in practice. He stated that although the council has been continuously working on this matter, the practice of various line ministries bypassing the council and operating projects through NGOs/INGOs continues.

Regarding monitoring and evaluation, information was provided that the council conducts project-based on-site monitoring through joint teams comprising thematic experts, financial experts, representatives of relevant ministries, and the council. He also pointed out the reality that although hundreds of projects were evaluated in the last fiscal year alone, the council does not have sufficient human resources, technical capacity, budget, and infrastructure.

On the question of financial transparency of NGOs/INGOs, he stated that in the case of international NGOs, almost one hundred percent audit and progress reports are received, while in the case of national NGOs, regular reports are received only from a limited number of organizations that receive foreign assistance. He stated that complete details of foreign funds entering without the council's approval and agreement do not come to the council's access. He stated that a situation exists where funds are being spent from various sources without the council's knowledge based on audit reports, indicating a serious gap in the regulatory system.

Although monitoring of expenditure at the local level is done through social auditing, public hearings, review meetings, information boards, websites, and the system of prior consent by local governments, it was clarified that exact data of NGOs/INGOs operating services by bypassing the council is not available with the council. It was acknowledged that in such a situation, the council also does not have adequate legal basis to take action. The statement reflects that monitoring of NGOs/INGOs involved in policy formulation, advocacy, and software-oriented activities is also done based on project proposals, objectives, and indicators, but the practice of mandatory sharing of surveys, mapping, or data collection with the council does not exist, and a definitive assessment of the risks arising from that could also not be made.

Regarding the entry of foreign experts, consultants, and expatriates, it was noted that although the council plays a role in the process of recommending work agreement, labor approval, and non-tourist visa, sensitive areas such as borders, security, energy, and essential infrastructure do not fall within the council's direct area of work. The statement mentions that the practice of monitoring expenditure at religious, community, and social institutions is also done through thematic experts during the course of project-based evaluation.

He has made a clear demand that the Social Welfare Council needs additional legal powers such as licensing, suspension, financial freeze, and investigation powers for effective regulation. He has pointed out as his conclusion that the non-implementation of the Social Welfare Act, 2049, is the biggest weakness of the current system. He stated that the influence of NGOs/INGOs appears most sensitive in the social sector, and that an overall assessment of the risks related to financial, policy, security, and political influence is still in process.
